title: Add and subtract fractions word problems (same denominator)
description: Practice solving word problems by adding and subtracting fractions. The fractions used in these problems have like (common) denominators.
topic_path: Adding and subtracting fractions: word problems
Add and subtract fractions word problems (same denominator)

assessment_items.question_content:
Lincoln wants to mix $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of a liter of red paint with $\dfrac{7}{8}$ of a liter of blue paint to make purple paint.

**How many liters of purple paint will Lincoln make by mixing the two colors?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] liters
hints:
To find the amount of $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$ paint, we can add the liters of $\redD{\text{red}}$ paint to the liters of $\blueD{\text{blue}}$ paint:

$\redD{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Lincoln will make ${\dfrac{10}{8}}$ liters of $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$ paint.

This can also be written as ${\dfrac{5}{4}}$ liters or ${1\dfrac{1}{4}}$ liters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Travis just started high school, and he wants to manage his time wisely.  He schedules $\dfrac{3}{2}$ hours each day for homework, $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of an hour each day for chores at home, and $\dfrac{17}{2}$ hours each day for sleep.

** How many hours each day are scheduled for these activities?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] hours
hints:
To find out the total hours Travis has scheduled for these activities, we can add the fractions:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{2}} + \greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{17}{2}}$

Travis schedules ${\dfrac{21}{2}}$ hours each day for these activities.

This can also be written as ${10\dfrac{1}{2}}$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bjorn is baking his favorite dessert.  The recipe calls for $\dfrac{5}{8}$ of a stick of butter, but he wants to double the recipe.  

**How much butter will Bjorn need?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] sticks
hints:
To find out how many sticks of butter Bjorn needs, we can add the fraction $\dfrac{5}{8}$ twice:

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{8}} + \greenD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

Bjorn will need  $\dfrac{{10}}{8}$ of a stick of butter.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{{5}}{4}$ sticks or ${1\dfrac{1}{4}}$ sticks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tyler and Katie started a lemonade stand to raise money.  They donated $\dfrac{2}{10}$ of their profits to their school library, $\dfrac{1}{10}$ to the animal shelter, and $\dfrac{4}{10}$ to the food bank.  They saved the rest to buy materials for their next project.

**What fraction of their profits did Tyler and Katie donate?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find out what fraction of the profits were donated, we can add the fractions:

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{10}} + \green{\dfrac{1}{10}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$
Tyler and Katie donated $\dfrac{{7}}{10}$ of their profits.

assessment_items.question_content:
One glass of milk gives you $\dfrac{3}{10}$ of the recommended amount of calcium per day.  

**$3$ glasses of milk will give you what fraction of the recommended amount of calcium per day?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find the fraction of recommended calcium from $3$ glasses of milk, we can add the fraction you will get from each glass of milk $3$ times:


$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}} + \green{\dfrac{3}{10}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$
If you drink $3$ glasses of milk a day, you will get $\dfrac{{9}}{10}$of the recommended amount of calcium.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joanna is carrying her books to school in her backpack.  Her library book weighs $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of a kilogram, her science book weighs $\dfrac{11}{8}$ kilograms, and her math book weighs $\dfrac{5}{8}$ kilograms. 

 **How much do Joanna's books weigh altogether?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilograms
hints:
To find out how many kilograms the books weigh altogether, we can add their weights:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

Joanna's books weigh $\dfrac{{19}}{8}$ kilograms.

This can also be written as ${2\dfrac{3}{8}}$ kilograms.

assessment_items.question_content:
A child's ladder is made of $3$ sections.  Each section is $\dfrac{3}{4}$ meters long. 

 **How long is the ladder when all $3$ sections are extended to make one ladder?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
To find out the total length of the ladder, we can add the lengths of the $3$ sections:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}} + \greenD{\dfrac{3}{4}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

The length of the child's ladder is $\dfrac{{9}}{4}$ meters.

This can also be written as ${2\dfrac{1}{4}}$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Winnie and Kevin like to create their own triathlon courses to challenge each other.  Last weekend, Winnie created a course that included a swim of $\dfrac{5}{4}$ kilometers, a bike ride of $\dfrac{95}{4}$ kilometers, and a run of $\dfrac{21}{4}$ kilometers.

**How long was the course Winnie created?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers
hints:
To find the total course length, we can add the fractions:

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{4}} + \greenD{\dfrac{95}{4}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{21}{4}}$

Winnie's course was $\dfrac{{121}}{4}$ kilometers long.

This can also be written as ${30\dfrac{1}{4}}$ kilometers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sven is reading a book while on vacation.  So far, he has read $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of the book. He wants to finish $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of the book by the end of vacation.

**What fraction of the book does Sven want to read between now and the end of vacation?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find the fraction of the book that Sven wants to read, we can subtract the fraction he has read from the total amount he wants to read:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{6}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$

Sven wants to read another $\dfrac{2}{6}$ of his book while he is on vacation.

We can also write this as $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of his book.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kira worked $\dfrac{87}{100}$ of the problems on her math test in the first hour. 

After the second hour, she had worked $\dfrac{97}{100}$ of the problems.

**What fraction of the problems did she work in the second hour?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find what fraction of the test Kira finished in the second hour, we can subtract the fraction that she had finished after $1$ hour from the fraction that she had finished after $2$ hours:

$\blueD{\dfrac{97}{100}} - \greenD{\dfrac{87}{100}}$

Kira finished $\dfrac{10}{100}$ of the problems in the second hour.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of the problems.

assessment_items.question_content:
James and Karlee caught a lizard in their backyard.  Its length from head to the end of the tail is $\dfrac{3}{5}$ meters, and the length of its tail is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ meters.

**What is the length of the lizard without the tail?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
To find the length of the lizard without the tail, we can subtract the length of the tail from the total length of the lizard.  

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{5}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$
The length of the lizard without the tail is $\dfrac{2}{5}$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pedro is supposed to practice piano for $\dfrac{4}{6}$ of an hour every day.  Today, he has practiced for $\dfrac{2}{6}$ of an hour.

**What fraction of an hour does he still need to practice?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find how much more time Pedro needs to practice, we can subtract the amount of time he has practiced from the total time he needs:

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{6}} - \greenD{\dfrac{2}{6}}$

Pedro still needs to practice $\dfrac{2}{6}$ of an hour.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an hour.

assessment_items.question_content:
Isa is allowed to play video games for $\dfrac{5}{3}$ hours each day.  She has already played for $\dfrac{4}{3}$ hours today.

**What fraction of an hour does Isa have left to play video games today?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find how much more time Isa has to play, we can subtract the amount of time she has already played from the total time she is allowed.  

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{3}} - \greenD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$
Isa still has $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an hour left to play video games.

assessment_items.question_content:
Together, Lulu and Jessica ate $\dfrac{7}{10}$ of a pizza.  Lulu ate $\dfrac{4}{10}$ of the pizza.

 **What fraction of the pizza did Jessica eat?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find how much pizza Jessica ate, we can subtract the amount of pizza Lulu ate from the total both Jessica and Lulu ate together.  

$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}} - \greenD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Stacy is measuring milk to make pancake batter.  The recipe calls for $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of a jug of milk.  She has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a jug now.  

**How much more milk does she need?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] of a jug
hints:
To find the amount of milk that is needed, we can subtract the amount of milk Stacy has from the amount she needs:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$

Stacy needs $\dfrac{2}{4}$ of a jug more milk to make her pancake batter.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a jug.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the heights of three different plants.

Type of Plant | Height (meters) 
- | - | -
Tomato | $\dfrac58$
Pepper | $\dfrac38$
Bean | $\dfrac28$

**What is the height of the tomato plant and the bean plant combined?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meter
hints:
To find the height of tomato plant and the bean plant combined, we can add. 

$\redD{\dfrac{5}{8}} + \blueD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$
The height of the tomato plant and the bean plant combined is $\dfrac78\text{ m}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the amount of apples three friends collected.

 ${}$ | Amount (bucket) 
- | - | -
Fred | $\dfrac56$
Taylor | $\dfrac46$
Amir | $\dfrac16$

**How many buckets of apples did Fred and Taylor pick combined?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] buckets
hints:
To find the amount of apples Fred and Taylor picked combined, we can add. 

$\redD{\dfrac{5}{6}} + \blueD{\dfrac{4}{6}}$
The amount of apples Fred and Taylor picked combined is $\dfrac96$ of a bucket, or $1 \dfrac12$ buckets.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the amount of cake $3$ friends ate.

 ${}$ | Amount eaten
- | - | -
Truett | $\dfrac3{10}$
Charlie | $\dfrac5{10}$
Kathryn | $\dfrac1{10}$

**How much cake did all three friends eat combined?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] cake
hints:
To find the amount of cake they ate, we can add.

$\redD{\dfrac{3}{10}} + \blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}} + \greenD{\dfrac1{10}}$
The amount of cake the three friends ate combined is $\dfrac9{10}$ of the cake.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the distance Veronica ran over a week.

Day | Length {km}
- | - | -
Monday| $\dfrac8{4}$
Tuesday | $\dfrac7{4}$
Thursday | $\dfrac3{4}$
Friday | $\dfrac5{4}$

**How many kilometers did Veronica run on Tuesday and Friday?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] kilometers
hints:
To find the length Veronica ran, we can add.

$\redD{\dfrac{7}{4}} + \blueD{\dfrac{5}{4}}$
Veronica ran $\dfrac{12}{4}$ of a kilometer on Tuesday and Friday, or $3$ kilometers.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the distance Shannon ran over a week.

Day | Length {km}
- | - | -
Tuesday| $\dfrac5{2}$
Wednesday | $\dfrac6{2}$
Friday | $\dfrac4{2}$
Saturday | $\dfrac1{2}$

**How many more kilometers did Shannon run on Friday than on Saturday?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] kilometers
hints:
To find the difference Shannon ran, we can subtract.

$\blueD{\dfrac{4}{2}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$
Shannon ran $\dfrac{3}{2}$ of kilometer more on Friday than on Saturday.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the heights of three different plants.

Type of Plant | Height (meters) 
- | - | -
Corn | $\dfrac{19}8$
Pepper | $\dfrac58$
Squash | $\dfrac78$

**How much taller is the tallest plant than the shortest plant?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
To find the difference between the two plants, we can subtract.

$\blueD{\dfrac{19}{8}} - \greenD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$
The difference between the tallest and shortest plant is $\dfrac{14}8\text{ m}$, or $1\dfrac34\text{ m}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shows the amount of juice $3$ friends drank. 

 ${}$ | Amount (liters) 
- | - | -
Owen | $\dfrac5{10}$
Nora | $\dfrac8{10}$
John | $\dfrac3{10}$

**How much more juice did Nora drink than Owen?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] liter
hints:
To find out how much more juice Nora drank, we can subtract.

$\blueD{\dfrac{8}{10}} - \greenD{\dfrac{5}{10}}$
Nora drank $\dfrac3{10}$ of a liter more juice than Owen.
